steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The passage says store context may 'moderate' the spacing effect — in research, moderate means to reduce or weaken. The original effect: products spaced far apart are perceived as more valuable. If store context moderates this, the spacing effect should be weaker at an inexpensive store (Forever 21) than at an upscale store (Tiffany & Co.).

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• "At both Tiffany & Co. and Forever 21, participants judged jewelry spaced far apart to be less valuable than jewelry spaced close together, but the difference in perceived value was significantly greater at Tiffany & Co. than at Forever 21.": shows a uniform spacing effect across both stores — no moderation at all. ✗
  • "When jewelry was spaced far apart, participants judged the jewelry at Tiffany & Co. to be more valuable than the jewelry at Forever 21, but when jewelry was spaced close together, participants judged the jewelry at Tiffany & Co. to be less valuable than the jewelry at Forever 21.": shows the spacing effect stronger at the inexpensive store — the opposite of moderation. ✗
  • "At Tiffany & Co., participants judged jewelry spaced far apart to be substantially more valuable than jewelry spaced close together, but at Forever 21, participants judged jewelry spaced far apart to be only slightly more valuable than jewelry spaced close together.": shows a substantial spacing effect at Tiffany & Co. but only a slight one at Forever 21 — the effect is weakened at the inexpensive store, directly supporting moderation. ✓
  • "Participants judged jewelry spaced far apart at Tiffany & Co. to be similar in value to jewelry spaced far apart at Forever 21, but participants judged jewelry spaced close together at Tiffany & Co. to be more valuable than jewelry spaced close together at Forever 21.": reverses the spacing relationship entirely — products close together are judged more valuable, contradicting the stated effect. ✗
That matches the correct answer — C is correct.
